name: Kuwaiti Arabic

ancestors.notes: Gulf Arabic base ‚Äî inherits *q‚Üí[…°], velar affrication, interdental retention and emphatic spreading.

notes: Kuwaiti Arabic ‚Äî the best-documented Gulf variety. Inherits the Gulf features: *q ‚Üí […°] (with [q] a formal/MSA candidate); velar affrication /k/‚Üí[t É] and /…°/‚Üí[d í] before high front /i, iÀê/; interdentals /Œ∏ √∞ √∞À§/ retained. Kuwaiti-specific: Matar (1969), as reported in Alshammari 2026 p.1335, describes /…°/ ‚Üí [d í] adjacent to front vowels, sometimes across an intervening /r, l/ (e.g. qariÀêb‚Üíd íiriÀêb), and in emphatic contexts (√∞À§iÀêg ‚Üí √∞À§iÀêd í) ‚Äî the non-adjacent trigger is NOT modelled (see GULF_G_AFFRICATION notes). The 2nd-person-feminine-singular suffix affricates to -t É (kashkasha), a morphological process outside base-orthography scope. Heavy English influence (post-1950s oil era): /p/, /v/, /≈ã/ well-established from loanwords. Input contract: fully diacritised (tashkeel) Arabic.

sources.author: Alshammari, K. K.
sources.title: An Optimality-Theoretic Account of Velar Affrication in Northern Najdi Arabic
sources.publisher: Journal of Language Teaching and Research 17(4)
sources.notes: Read in full (open-access PDF). Reports (p.1335) Matar's (1969) study of Kuwaiti /…°/‚Üí[d í] affrication adjacent to front vowels and in emphatic context, and Johnstone (1967) on the lexicalisation of velar affrication in Eastern Arabic.

sources.author: Al-Balushi, R.
sources.title: Omani Arabic: More than a Dialect
sources.notes: Read the phonology sections. Notes /q/‚Üí[…£] in some Kuwaiti varieties (e.g. …£ir≈° 'shark' < qir≈°), cross-referenced with Eastern Arabian /q/ reflexes (p.89).

location.notes: Kuwait City. A national variety within the Gulf continuum. [Wikidata Q35178 P625]

stress.notes: Arabic stress is QUANTITY-SENSITIVE: it falls on a syllable because that syllable is heavy, not because of any orthographic ending, so no ending table can express it. The cascade (Ryding, A Reference Grammar of MSA, CUP 2005, ¬ß2.3; Watson, The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic, OUP 2002, ch.3): a superheavy final syllable (CVÀêC/CVCC) takes the stress (kiÀàtaÀêb); otherwise a heavy penult (CVÀê/CVC) does (muÀàdarris); otherwise the antepenult (Ààmadrasa). max_onset=1 because Arabic onsets are obligatory and hold exactly one consonant ‚Äî a medial cluster splits, closing the preceding syllable, and that boundary is what makes the penult of mudarris heavy. INPUT CONTRACT: weight is only recoverable from a complete transcription, so this requires diacritized input; an undiacritized skeleton has no vowels to weigh. The registered wikipron gold carries no stress marks, but the benchmark strips stress from both sides by default, so this costs no PER.
